findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` to the Files section and specify that `"metrics"` must be added to the `individual` frozenset inside `_routable_subcommands()`.
evidence: Plan Files section lists no edit to tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py

Plan prose: 'If the subcommand-routability test tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py (its _routable_subcommands()) enumerates commands, ensure metrics is recognized there so that coverage check passes.'
_routable_subcommands() hardcodes individual = frozenset({'init','scratch','delete','fsck','fsck-recover','bridge-probe','grounding-info','audit'}) — 'metrics' absent
test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set asserts known - routable == frozenset() — a new help/*.txt without a routable arm fails this assertion
findings.finding: tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py is an unenumerated edit target: its _routable_subcommands() builds the routable set by unioning named frozensets plus a hardcoded 'individual' frozenset. Adding 'metrics' as an explicit 'if sub == "metrics":' arm in _dispatch() — which is what the plan specifies — means 'metrics' will appear in known_subcommands() (via the new .txt file) but NOT in _routable_subcommands(), causing test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set to fail with 'pinned help text but not routable'. The plan mentions this file only in prose ('ensure metrics is recognized there') but omits it from the Files section.
findings.location: Files section / tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py
scenarios: Developer adds metrics.txt and metrics if-arm in _dispatch but does not update test_help_overview_coverage.py → test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set fails in CI with 'pinned help text but not routable: [metrics]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` — add `"metrics"` to the `individual` frozenset in `_routable_subcommands()` (line ~51).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the Files section that `"metrics"` must be added to the `individual` frozenset in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py`'s `_routable_subcommands()` (mirroring how `"init"`, `"scratch"`, etc. are listed there).
evidence: test_help_overview_coverage.py lines 50-61: `individual = frozenset({'init', 'scratch', 'delete', 'fsck', 'fsck-recover', 'bridge-probe', 'grounding-info', 'audit'})` — `metrics` absent
Plan: 'add an explicit `if sub == "metrics":` arm in `_dispatch()`' — individual arms in _dispatch are NOT auto-detected by _routable_subcommands()
Plan: 'ensure `metrics` is recognized there so that coverage check passes' — mechanism unspecified
findings.finding: The plan says 'ensure `metrics` is recognized' in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` (`_routable_subcommands()`), but does not specify the concrete mechanism: `_routable_subcommands()` builds its set from named frozensets unioned with a hardcoded `individual` frozenset. A `_dispatch` `if sub == "metrics":` arm is not automatically detected — `"metrics"` must be explicitly added to `individual` in that test file. The plan's 'ensure... recognized' phrasing is ambiguous about whether the executor must edit the test's `individual` frozenset.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` description; and the bullet: 'If the subcommand-routability test ... enumerates commands, ensure `metrics` is recognized there'
scenarios: Executor adds the dispatch arm but doesn't update the test's `individual` frozenset; `test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set` fails because `metrics` has pinned help text but is not in the routable set.
Executor adds `metrics` to a frozenset (e.g., _READS_INIT_ONLY) instead of `individual`, causing incorrect initialization behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files or Approach section: 'Add `"metrics"` to the `individual` frozenset inside `_routable_subcommands()` in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py`.'

findings.finding: `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` is an unenumerated consumer: its `_routable_subcommands()` function hardcodes the `individual` frozenset (init, scratch, delete, fsck, fsck-recover, bridge-probe, grounding-info, audit). Adding `metrics` as a dedicated `if sub == 'metrics':` dispatch arm (not a frozenset member) means `test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set` will fail — `metrics` appears in `known_subcommands()` via `metrics.txt` but is absent from the returned routable set.
findings.location: Files section / `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` edit + `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` (not listed)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'metrics'` to the `individual` frozenset in `_routable_subcommands()` inside `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `'metrics'` to the hardcoded `individual` frozenset in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` `_routable_subcommands()` (the plan currently only says 'ensure metrics is recognized there' without naming this edit).
evidence: tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py lines 50-61: individual = frozenset({'init','scratch','delete','fsck','fsck-recover','bridge-probe','grounding-info','audit'}) — hardcoded, no auto-discovery
test line 78: assert routable - known == frozenset() / line 81: assert known - routable == frozenset() — both directions enforced
plan says only 'ensure metrics is recognized there so that coverage check passes' without naming the specific frozenset edit
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding a `if sub == 'metrics':` dispatch arm (not a frozenset), then says 'ensure metrics is recognized there so that coverage check passes' — but the coverage test's `_routable_subcommands()` has a HARDCODED `individual` frozenset (lines 50-61 of test_help_overview_coverage.py) that must be explicitly extended with `'metrics'`. The plan does not name this concrete edit, so a developer following the plan literally would add the dispatch arm but NOT update `individual`, causing `test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set` to fail (`pinned help text but not routable: ['metrics']`).
findings.location: Files section / 'If the subcommand-routability test … ensure metrics is recognized there'
scenarios: Developer adds help/metrics.txt (making it 'known') and the dispatch arm, but doesn't update the hardcoded `individual` frozenset; test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set fails with 'pinned help text but not routable: [metrics]'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add: `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py` — add `'metrics'` to the `individual` frozenset inside `_routable_subcommands()` (lines 50-61).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the JSON output contract: either (a) update the plan to reflect that `lens` must be read from `MetricSpec` (not `MetricValue`) and included explicitly by the render loop, or (b) note that `MetricValue` needs a `lens` field — because the current `MetricValue` dataclass has no `lens` field.
evidence: Plan: '{<id>: {"lens","source","confidence","value"} | {"unavailable": ...}}'
registry.py MetricValue dataclass (lines 65-77): fields are value, source, confidence, metric_id — no lens field
MetricSpec (lines 48-62) carries the lens field — it is not propagated into MetricValue by evaluate()
findings.finding: The plan's JSON output contract for a value entry specifies `{"lens", "source", "confidence", "value"}`, but the actual `MetricValue` dataclass (registry.py lines 65-77) has fields `value`, `source`, `confidence`, `metric_id` — there is NO `lens` field on `MetricValue`. The `lens` field lives on `MetricSpec`, not on the result type. If `metrics.py` serializes a `MetricValue` directly using its fields, the JSON will emit `metric_id` (not `lens`), breaking the plan's stated machine contract.
findings.location: Approach / Output formats section — JSON format specification
scenarios: Developer naively serializes MetricValue fields into the JSON output and emits metric_id instead of lens, or omits lens entirely — the machine contract diverges from the plan's specification.
findings.suggested_fix: In `metrics.py`'s render loop, produce the value entry as `{"lens": spec.lens, "source": result.source, "confidence": result.confidence, "value": result.value}` (pulling `lens` from `spec`, not from the `MetricValue`). Update the plan's Approach section to make this explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the test description (and implement in `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`) that the test must `import rebar.metrics` (the package, not just `rebar.metrics.registry`) before reading `REGISTRY` for the right-hand-side set, and add an explicit guard assertion `assert len(REGISTRY) > 0` to confirm hydration succeeded before the superset check.
evidence: Plan states (Approach): 'metrics.py must `import rebar.metrics` (the package — running its `__init__`, which hydrates `REGISTRY`)'
Plan states (AC1): 'asserts `set(output["metrics"].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}`'
ABSENCE: the test file description never mentions importing `rebar.metrics` (package) before reading `rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY` for the right-hand side of the assertion
If REGISTRY is empty in the test process, `set(output["metrics"].keys()) >= set()` is unconditionally True regardless of CLI output
findings.finding: The AC1 assertion `set(output["metrics"].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}` can trivially pass (vacuously true as `set() >= set()`) if the test file imports `rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY` directly without first importing the `rebar.metrics` package, leaving REGISTRY unpopulated. The plan specifies the hydration requirement for `metrics.py` but does NOT extend it to `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`, creating a fixture-invalidity defect where the proving command passes without exercising the claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / Tests section / `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`
scenarios: A test that does `from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY` without `import rebar.metrics` gets an empty REGISTRY; the superset assertion passes even if the CLI emits zero metrics, giving a false green.
A future reader of the test cannot tell whether the non-empty REGISTRY invariant is enforced.
findings.suggested_fix:
In `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`, add:
```python
import rebar.metrics  # hydrate REGISTRY via package __init__
from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY

def test_metrics_json_coverage(tmp_store):
    assert len(REGISTRY) > 0, "REGISTRY must be non-empty after import rebar.metrics"
    result = subprocess.run(["rebar", "metrics", "--since", "2026-01-01", "--until", "2026-07-01", "--output", "json"], ...)
    data = json.loads(result.stdout)
    assert set(data["metrics"].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}
```
Also add this guard requirement to the plan's Tests section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `evaluate(spec, context)` (or the render loop in `metrics.py`) wraps each metric computation in a try/except and emits an `unavailable` entry with the exception message as `reason` on failure, so one broken reader never crashes the whole command.
evidence: 'emitting value-or-`unavailable` per metric' — implies graceful handling but does not specify exception catching
ABSENCE: no try/except or error-surfacing contract around `evaluate(spec, context)` calls is stated
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when a metric's `compute()` callable raises an exception — whether it is caught and emitted as `unavailable` or allowed to propagate and crash the command.
findings.location: Approach section / `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py`
scenarios: A reader's compute() raises an unexpected exception (e.g., file read error, parse error); the command crashes with a traceback instead of emitting an `unavailable` entry.
A partially-evaluated run emits some metrics then aborts, giving the caller incomplete JSON.
findings.suggested_fix: In the render loop: `try: result = evaluate(spec, ctx) except Exception as e: result = {'unavailable': {'reason': str(e), 'accruing_since': None}}` — emit that instead of propagating.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the render loop description that `spec.lens` must be read directly from the `MetricSpec` (not from `evaluate()`'s return value) to populate the `lens` key in each JSON value entry — since `MetricValue` carries no `lens` attribute.
evidence: Plan: '`{<id>: {"lens","source","confidence","value"} | {"unavailable": {"reason","accruing_since"}}}`'
registry.py MetricValue dataclass fields: `value: Any`, `source: str`, `confidence: str`, `metric_id: str | None` — no `lens`
registry.py MetricSpec has `lens: str` but MetricValue does not inherit or carry it
evaluate() returns MetricValue(value=result, source=spec.source, confidence=spec.confidence, metric_id=spec.id) — lens absent
findings.finding: The JSON output schema specifies `{"lens","source","confidence","value"}` for a value entry, but `MetricValue` (the object `evaluate()` returns) has no `lens` field — `lens` is on `MetricSpec`, not `MetricValue`. The plan's render loop description ('calls evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec, emitting value-or-unavailable') never mentions fetching `spec.lens` separately to populate this key, leaving the schema unachievable with `evaluate()` alone.
findings.location: Approach / Output formats section — JSON schema definition: `{"lens","source","confidence","value"}`
scenarios: Implementer calls evaluate(spec, ctx) and serialises the returned MetricValue directly — the 'lens' key is missing from every value entry, breaking the machine contract and the AC assertion.
Automated consumer of the JSON output that reads .metrics[id].lens gets KeyError or None on every metric.
findings.suggested_fix: In the render loop, for each spec emit: `{'lens': spec.lens, 'source': result.source, 'confidence': result.confidence, 'value': result.value}` (reading `spec.lens` from the spec, not from the `MetricValue` returned by `evaluate()`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `metrics.py`'s render loop handles exceptions raised by `spec.compute(context)` — either require `metrics.py` to wrap each `evaluate()` call in a per-metric try/except that maps raised exceptions to an `Unavailable`-equivalent entry, or update the AC's definition of 'unpopulated' to cover exception-raised unavailability.
evidence: registry.py `evaluate()` has no try/except: `result = spec.compute(context)`; returns `Unavailable` only when `result is None`
git_metrics.py `_git()`: `subprocess.run(["git", *args], ..., check=True)` — raises `CalledProcessError` if git is unavailable or repo has no commits
git_metrics.py `_read_cap()`: `(Path(repo_root) / _LIMIT_FILE).read_text(...)` — raises `FileNotFoundError` if the limit file is absent
AC: 'an unpopulated metric shows `unavailable`; `--output text` lists every metric id' — both require every compute to either return a value or None, never raise
findings.finding: The render loop calls `registry.evaluate(spec, context)` which in turn calls `spec.compute(context)` with no exception handling. Reader modules (`git_metrics.py`, `event_metrics.py`, `sidecar_metrics.py`) make subprocess calls (`subprocess.run(..., check=True)`) and file-system reads that raise `subprocess.CalledProcessError`, `FileNotFoundError`, etc. when the environment is incomplete (e.g., the temp store in CI has no git history, no `.github/module-size-limit.txt`). These exceptions propagate out of `evaluate()` and crash the command; they do NOT produce an `Unavailable`. The plan's AC says 'an unpopulated metric shows `unavailable`' and the test exercises a temp store, so a crashing compute function would fail the AC rather than returning the expected unavailable entry.
findings.location: Approach section / 'The context object' paragraph; AC line: 'an unpopulated metric shows `unavailable`'
scenarios: In the CLI subprocess test against a fresh temp store: git_metrics compute functions are called against a repo with no commits or no `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, raising an exception that propagates through evaluate() and crashes the command with exit non-zero and no JSON output.
In any real repo missing optional infrastructure files (e.g., a repo that has never had the module-size-limit file), `rebar metrics` crashes instead of reporting unavailable.
findings.suggested_fix: In `metrics.py`, wrap each `evaluate()` call: `try: result = evaluate(spec, ctx) except Exception as exc: result = Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)`. This makes exception-throwing computes indistinguishable from None-returning ones in the CLI output and satisfies the AC's 'unavailable' contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the render loop should wrap each `evaluate()` call in a try/except to catch compute exceptions and emit them as `{"unavailable": {"reason": "<exc>", ...}}`, or whether `evaluate()` itself should be extended to catch exceptions — since the current `evaluate()` has no exception guard and compute functions can raise.
evidence: registry.py evaluate() lines 103-122: `result = spec.compute(context)` — no try/except around the compute call
git_metrics.py: compute functions call subprocess.check_output (can raise CalledProcessError, FileNotFoundError)
Plan: 'evaluates every MetricSpec in the registry' — no mention of exception handling
AC: 'an unpopulated metric shows unavailable' — only the None-return path is tested, not the exception path
findings.finding: The plan's render loop ('calls evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec, emitting value-or-unavailable') specifies no fallback for the case where `spec.compute(context)` raises an exception rather than returning `None`. The `evaluate()` function in `registry.py` has no try/except — exceptions propagate. The AC only covers the `None`→`Unavailable` path; a subprocess error, missing file, or other runtime exception in a compute function will crash the entire `rebar metrics` command rather than emitting a per-metric `unavailable` entry.
findings.location: Approach section — render loop description; AC line: 'an unpopulated metric shows `unavailable`'
scenarios: Git is not available in the test environment; `git_metrics` compute raises `FileNotFoundError`; the whole command exits non-zero with a traceback instead of reporting those metrics as unavailable.
A sidecar file is corrupt; the sidecar_metrics compute raises a JSON decode error; the command crashes mid-render.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling when a `compute` callable raises an unexpected exception (as opposed to returning an `unavailable` object). If one reader throws, the plan gives no indication whether `evaluate()` catches it, renders it as unavailable, or lets it propagate and crash the entire `rebar metrics` invocation.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The context object' and 'Output formats'; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'evaluate() wraps each compute(ctx) call in a bare except (or Exception catch); any raised exception is rendered as {"unavailable": {"reason": "error: <str(exc)}"}} so one reader failure degrades gracefully rather than aborting the command.'

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path handling to the plan for `metrics`: specify that invalid date formats, inverted `--since`/`--until` ranges, missing required args, and unrecognized `--output` values each produce a clear error message and a non-zero exit code (and add at least one AC or test assertion covering an invalid-input case).
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of date-parse error handling, inverted-range validation, or argparse error for bad --output value
AC only tests the happy path: 'exits 0 and the emitted .metrics map contains an entry for EVERY id'
'parses --since/--until and --output json|text' — parsing is named but error cases are not
findings.finding: The plan does not specify validation or error handling for invalid user inputs: malformed `--since`/`--until` date strings, an inverted range (`--until` before `--since`), omitted required args, or an unrecognized `--output` value.
findings.location: Approach section / Tests section / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: `rebar metrics --since foo --until 2026-07-01` — plan doesn't say whether this exits with a clear error message and non-zero code or crashes with a traceback.
`rebar metrics --since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` (inverted range) — undefined behavior.
`rebar metrics --output csv` — unrecognized format, undefined behavior.
Omitting `--since` or `--until` — undefined behavior (required vs optional?).
findings.suggested_fix: Use `argparse` choices for `--output` (`choices=['json','text']`) for free validation; add a post-parse check `if since > until: parser.error('--since must be before --until')`; wrap date parsing in try/except with `parser.error()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `metrics.py` calls `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output()` for `--output` (either add a `'metrics'` profile to `PROFILES` for default=json / allowed={json,text}, or pass explicit `allowed`/`default` kwargs) rather than hand-rolling its own flag parser.
evidence: Plan says 'parses `--since/--until` and `--output json|text`' with no mention of parse_output()

output.py is self-described as 'Single source of truth for rebar's --output / -o flag' with 20+ confirmed call sites across signing.py, bridge.py, gates.py, reads_cli.py, validate.py, bridge_fsck.py, field_reads.py, _cli/__init__.py, delete.py, composer.py, transition.py, claim.py, fsck.py, idea.py
Existing PROFILES: {'reader': ('json', ('json','llm')), 'ready': ('text', ('text','llm','json')), 'report': ('text', ('text','json'))} — none matches default=json with allowed={json,text}
findings.finding: The plan says `metrics.py` will parse `--output json|text` without referencing the existing `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output()` utility, which has 20+ call sites and is the project's single source of truth for `--output` flag parsing. The metrics command wants default=json with allowed={json, text} — no existing profile covers this combination — so either a new 'metrics' profile should be added to PROFILES or `parse_output` should be called with explicit `allowed`/`default` kwargs. Leaving this unspecified risks a hand-rolled ad-hoc parser that duplicates functionality already in the codebase.
findings.location: Approach section / Output formats paragraph; src/rebar/_engine_support/output.py PROFILES dict
scenarios: Developer implementing metrics.py hand-rolls `if '--output' in argv` parsing, duplicating OutputFormatError handling, equals-form (`--output=json`) parsing, and last-occurrence semantics that parse_output already provides.
A future output format (e.g. 'llm') added globally via parse_output profiles is silently absent from metrics because metrics uses its own parser.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'metrics': ('json', ('json', 'text'))` to `PROFILES` in `output.py`, then in `metrics.py`: `from rebar._engine_support.output import OutputFormatError, parse_output; fmt, rest = parse_output(argv, 'metrics')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or strengthen AC5) that verifies `docs/cli-reference.md` is up-to-date by asserting the CI drift gate (`python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` produces no diff) passes as part of the acceptance suite.
evidence: "docs/cli-reference.md — do NOT hand-edit: it is GENERATED by scripts/gen_cli_reference.py (with a CI drift gate). After adding the help/overview text, REGENERATE it (python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py) and commit the result."

AC5 reads: 'the golden CLI-help snapshots are regenerated (and docs/cli-reference.md via gen_cli_reference.py), and the tests/golden/cli_help + help-overview-coverage suites pass' — the parenthetical mentions the regeneration but no criterion asserts the drift-gate CI check passes or that the committed file matches generator output.
findings.finding: The Files section explicitly describes regenerating `docs/cli-reference.md` via `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` and notes a CI drift gate, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that this regeneration was performed or that the drift gate passes — leaving a described deliverable uncovered.
findings.location: Files section ('docs/cli-reference.md — do NOT hand-edit...') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC5 covers golden/help-overview suites only)
scenarios: An implementer regenerates the goldens but forgets to commit the updated docs/cli-reference.md; AC5 still passes because only the test suites are asserted, and the drift gate silently fails in CI later.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 to read: '…and `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` produces no diff against the committed `docs/cli-reference.md` (the drift gate passes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the adapter-isolation rule and registry-hydration pattern in a persistent doc (e.g., add a section to AGENTS.md or a lightweight ADR) so future contributors extending `_commands/` know the constraints and their rationale without reading closed tickets.
evidence: 'Adapter isolation (portability guard from 1f77/538c): metrics.py must NOT import any rebar.metrics.adapters module.'
'REGISTRY is only fully populated after the reader modules run their registration side-effects... Importing rebar.metrics.registry ALONE does not trigger them.'
No mention of updating AGENTS.md, adding an ADR, or any persistent design doc capturing these constraints.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel architectural constraints — (1) the adapter-isolation portability guard (source-level scan asserting no `rebar.metrics.adapters` import in `metrics.py`) and (2) the registry hydration rule (must `import rebar.metrics` the package, not `rebar.metrics.registry` alone) — but neither is committed to a persistent design record (ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc). These are non-obvious invariants that any future contributor adding a new `_commands/*.py` file or refactoring imports must know about; the plan text itself is ephemeral.
findings.location: Plan § Approach — 'REGISTRY hydration' and 'Adapter isolation (portability guard)' paragraphs; AC bullet 3
scenarios: A future developer adds a second read command (e.g., `rebar trends`) and directly imports `rebar.metrics.registry` without the package, silently getting an empty registry with no test failure until the portability guard is explicitly extended to cover the new file.

A contributor adds a convenience import of an adapter inside `metrics.py` for a new feature, unaware of the portability constraint, breaking the architectural boundary — and only learns about it when the test fires, with no doc explaining the rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief `## Metrics subsystem constraints` section to AGENTS.md (or a `docs/adr/metrics-isolation.md`) noting: (1) any `_commands/*.py` that uses the metrics registry MUST `import rebar.metrics` (the package) to hydrate REGISTRY, NOT `rebar.metrics.registry` alone; (2) `_commands/metrics.py` (and any future metrics-adjacent command) must never import `rebar.metrics.adapters.*` — adapters reach the command only via REGISTRY/snapshot store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add failure-path tests to `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py` covering at minimum: (1) invalid date format → non-zero exit + meaningful error output; (2) `--since` after `--until` → defined behavior (error or empty range) tested; (3) missing `--since` or `--until` → non-zero exit; (4) unrecognized `--output` value → non-zero exit.
evidence: AC states only: exits 0 and emits correct structure; no AC or test bullet addresses non-zero exit on bad input
Plan's 'Tests' section: 'rebar metrics --since ... --until ... --output json exits 0 and emits a metrics map' — exclusively success-path
ABSENCE: no failure-path scenario specified for date parsing, range inversion, missing flags, or unknown output format
findings.finding: The testing plan covers only happy-path scenarios for the new user-facing `rebar metrics` CLI command. No test is specified for invalid or missing inputs: malformed date strings (`--since not-a-date`), inverted date range (`--since` after `--until`), missing required arguments (no `--since`/`--until`), or an unrecognized `--output` value (e.g. `--output csv`). For a new user-facing CLI flow, the absence of any failure-path test coverage is a MAJOR gap.
findings.location: Tests section and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: User passes `--since 2026-13-01` (invalid month) — no specified behavior or test
User omits `--since` — unclear if required; no test for error exit
User passes `--output csv` — no test that this produces a useful error and non-zero exit
User passes `--since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` (inverted range) — no test for defined behavior
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`, add parametrized subprocess tests for each failure case, asserting `returncode != 0` and that stderr/stdout contains a human-readable error message. Add corresponding ACs (e.g., 'invalid --since format exits non-zero with a date-format error message').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the JSON output schema (field names and their types/semantics) to a persistent location — either as a JSON Schema file, a dedicated section in `docs/cli-reference.md` (via the generator), or in `metrics.txt` help — so consumers and future implementors have a stable reference.
evidence: 'JSON (default) is the machine contract: {"since": <str>, "until": <str>, "metrics": {<id>: {"lens","source","confidence","value"} | {"unavailable": {"reason","accruing_since"}}, ...}}'
'docs/cli-reference.md — do NOT hand-edit: it is GENERATED by scripts/gen_cli_reference.py' — the generated reference captures help text, not JSON schema shape.
findings.finding: The machine-readable JSON output contract (the `{"since", "until", "metrics": {id: {"lens","source","confidence","value"} | {"unavailable": {"reason","accruing_since"}}}}` schema) is defined only in the ephemeral plan text. No persistent interface doc (schema file, OpenAPI fragment, or CLI-reference section) captures this shape, making it easy for future callers or the implementation to drift without detection.
findings.location: Plan § Output formats; AC bullet 1
scenarios: A consumer script parsing `rebar metrics --output json` uses `.metrics[id].confidence`; a future implementation change renames the field to `confidence_score` — no schema doc makes the breakage silent until runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named stakeholder (e.g., 'developer/operator inspecting project health') and at least one before/after workflow validation criterion to the plan (e.g., 'before: no single command to query metrics over a range; after: `rebar metrics --since … --until … --output json` returns structured results in < 2 s on a populated store').
evidence: Plan's 'What' section describes the mechanism ('parses --since/--until', 'renders the full metric registry') without naming who needs this or what workflow gap it closes.
All five ACs are structural correctness checks (exit 0, key set superset, no adapter import, help text, golden regen) — none frames a before/after user workflow or measurable outcome.
findings.finding: The plan does not name a specific user/stakeholder or describe the problem they face today, and no AC carries a before/after workflow comparison or operational metric target — only technical correctness assertions (exit 0, JSON structure, key set coverage).
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A reviewer or PM cannot validate whether the delivered command actually improves a developer's or operator's ability to inspect project health, because the plan provides no baseline state ('today, getting metrics requires X; after, the user can Y in one command') to compare against.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add one sentence: 'For developers and operators who need to query project health signals over a date range, this command provides a single structured entry point — replacing ad-hoc store inspection.' In the ACs, add: '[ ] Running `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output json` on a populated store completes in under 5 s and returns a non-empty metrics map suitable for downstream tooling.'

findings.finding: The JSON output schema for `--bucket` mode is unspecified. The plan says output is 'keyed by bucket' but does not define: (a) the bucket key format (ISO week string, ISO date, 'start/end' pair, etc.), (b) whether the top-level `since`/`until` still appear, or (c) whether each bucket value repeats the `{since, until, metrics}` envelope or uses a flatter structure. An executor must invent this schema, risking a mismatch with the AC5 assertion.
findings.location: Approach section — 'for --bucket, it builds one context per bucket sub-range … keying the output by bucket'; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Output formats section: 'When `--bucket` is supplied, the JSON envelope is `{"bucket_unit": "week", "buckets": [{"since": "<ISO-date>", "until": "<ISO-date>", "metrics": {…}}, …]}` — top-level `since`/`until` are omitted; each bucket repeats the per-metric structure.' Then update AC5 to assert `jq -e '.buckets | length >= 1' output.json`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'each reader already accepts since/until as ISO date bounds' as the mechanism justifying --bucket evaluation, but this is factually incorrect for `module_size_distribution` (registered with `ranged=False` in git_metrics.py), which ignores ctx.since/ctx.until entirely and always reflects the current working tree. With `--bucket week`, this metric emits identical values for every bucket — silent incorrect output — yet the AC for `--bucket week` only checks 'at least one bucket key exists' and would pass regardless, leaving the defect undetected.
findings.location: Approach section — '--bucket' paragraph ('each reader already accepts since/until as ISO date bounds'); AC line 5 ('--bucket week yields a per-bucket series for a metric with history')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the --bucket approach paragraph: 'Metrics whose compute ignores since/until (ranged=False, e.g. module_size_distribution) are snapshot metrics — the bucketing loop should emit them only for the first bucket (or under a `snapshot` key outside the bucket series) to avoid misleading duplication. AC5 should assert `output[bucket_0]["churn"]["value"] != output[bucket_1]["churn"]["value"]` (or similar) for at least one ranged metric, not merely that a bucket key is present.'

findings.finding: The `--bucket` sub-range splitting algorithm is referenced but never specified: no boundary arithmetic (how week/month boundaries are computed from `since`/`until`), no definition of what constitutes a 'bucket key' in JSON output (ISO week string? start-of-period date? `YYYY-WNN` format?), and no handling rule for partial periods at the start/end of the range.
findings.location: Approach section — `--bucket day|week|month` paragraph; AC '`--bucket week` yields a per-bucket series'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Buckets are calendar-aligned (ISO 8601): `day` → `YYYY-MM-DD`, `week` → `YYYY-WNN` (ISO week), `month` → `YYYY-MM`. Sub-ranges are `[period_start, min(period_end, until)]`; a partial period at range boundaries is included (not dropped). The first sub-range starts at `since`; the last ends at `until`.' Update the AC to assert the specific key format for `--bucket week`.

findings.finding: The `--bucket` JSON output shape is referenced across the plan ('keying the output by bucket', AC: 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key') but the bucket key format and the top-level JSON envelope for a bucketed response are never defined. An implementer does not know whether keys are ISO week-start dates (`2026-01-05`), week-number strings (`week-2`), ISO range strings (`2026-01-01/2026-01-07`), or something else — nor whether the top-level shape is `{"buckets": {"<key>": {"since","until","metrics":{...}}}}` or a flat object or a list.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'For --bucket, it builds one context per bucket sub-range'; ## Acceptance Criteria — last bullet; ## Output formats section

findings.finding: The plan's Approach and Output formats sections describe a `--output text` human-readable format (with a defined layout: `<id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value-or-"unavailable: <reason>">`) as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion covers the text output format or verifies it renders correctly.
findings.location: Output formats section ('`--output text`' paragraph) and Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: The `--output text` rendering path is described as a non-deferred deliverable with a specific, named format (one line per metric, fixed-width layout, `unavailable: <reason>` suffix), but no Acceptance Criterion validates it — no AC asserts `rebar metrics --output text` exits 0, produces the described layout, or is tested at all. A non-deferred goal without an end-state-observable proof violates E3(b).
findings.location: Approach section: 'Output formats. … `--output text` is a deterministic human summary — one line per metric in registry order …'; Tests section lists only `--output json` in the CLI test.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Tests: 'CLI test also invokes `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output text`, asserts exit 0, and asserts stdout contains one line per registered metric id matching the pattern `<id>  [` (checking id and bracket-delimited lens/source prefix).'
Add a corresponding AC: '`--output text` exits 0 and each registered metric id appears on its own line with `[<lens>/<source>]` present.'

findings.finding: The plan states the adapters package 'may not exist yet at this point in the stack' to justify the portability guard, but `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` already exists with `__init__.py`, `claude_transcripts.py`, and `github_actions.py`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Adapter isolation (portability guard from 1f77/538c)': 'Because that adapters package may not exist yet at this point in the stack'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sentence with: 'Because adapter modules are CI/harness-specific (e.g. `claude_transcripts.py`, `github_actions.py`), the guard is a source-level scan: the test reads `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` and asserts its source contains no `rebar.metrics.adapters` import, ensuring adapter-backed metrics reach the command only through REGISTRY.'

findings.finding: The plan describes two contradictory wiring actions for `metrics` in `_cli/__init__.py`: (1) 'add `metrics` to a reads-with-init grouping' (implying membership in a frozenset like `_READS_INIT_ONLY`) AND (2) 'add an explicit `if sub == "metrics":` arm'. If `metrics` is added to `_READS_INIT_ONLY`, the existing dispatch code at line 201 (`if sub in _READS_INIT_ONLY: … return reads.main([sub, *rest])`) fires FIRST and routes `metrics` to `reads.main` — the dedicated arm is never reached.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` description

findings.finding: The Output formats section specifies a concrete non-bucketed JSON schema (`{"since","until","metrics": {...}}`), but the Approach section describes `--bucket` as producing output "keyed by bucket" — implying a structurally different top-level shape. The two descriptions define incompatible output schemas for the same command without reconciling them, and the AC for `--bucket` ("JSON contains at least one bucket key") references a structure not defined in the Output formats section.
findings.location: Approach section ('--bucket' paragraph) vs. Output formats section (JSON schema) vs. AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Output formats: 'With `--bucket`, the JSON top-level is `{"buckets": [{"since": <bucket_start>, "until": <bucket_end>, "metrics": {<id>: ...}}, ...]}`, one entry per sub-range.' Update AC5 to assert `"buckets"` is a non-empty list with each entry containing a `"metrics"` map.

findings.finding: AC5's assertion 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key' is too weak to be meaningfully measurable — a single-bucket response (the entire range as one bucket) trivially satisfies it, and an `unavailable` result in every bucket satisfies it just as well as a real bucketed series. The criterion does not state a minimum bucket count, a non-`unavailable` value requirement, or a key-format constraint.
findings.location: AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Files section to enumerate all six new golden files for `metrics`: `metrics__help.err`, `metrics__help.exit`, `metrics__help.out`, `metrics__dashhelp.err`, `metrics__dashhelp.exit`, `metrics__dashhelp.out`.
evidence: Plan text: 'add metrics__help.out' — only one golden file named for the new subcommand
Observed pattern in tests/golden/cli_help/: every subcommand has both __help.{err,exit,out} AND __dashhelp.{err,exit,out} (6 files total)
Example: scratch__help.err, scratch__help.exit, scratch__help.out, scratch__dashhelp.err, scratch__dashhelp.exit, scratch__dashhelp.out all exist
findings.finding: The golden file enumeration for the new 'metrics' subcommand is incomplete. Every existing subcommand has six golden files: metrics__help.err, metrics__help.exit, metrics__help.out, metrics__dashhelp.err, metrics__dashhelp.exit, metrics__dashhelp.out. The plan names only 'metrics__help.out' — omitting the .err/.exit variants and the entire __dashhelp triplet.
findings.location: Files section: 'tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate ... and add metrics__help.out'
scenarios: A developer following the plan creates only metrics__help.out and misses the other 5 golden files, causing the golden-snapshot test suite to fail or leave undefined behavior for --help vs -h invocations.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
